title: SQL Lab 1: Second Highest Salary
short_description: Return the second highest distinct salary while handling duplicate salary values.
business_context: HR analytics often needs salary band calculations where duplicate values should not change the rank.
problem_statement: The employees table can contain multiple employees with the same salary. Return the second highest distinct salary. If only one distinct salary exists, the result should be NULL.
student_task: Write a single SQL query that returns one column named second_highest_salary.
learning_goals: Think in distinct values before ranking
Avoid ROW_NUMBER when the business asks for distinct salary bands
broken_code:
SELECT salary AS second_highest_salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;
production_logs: interview-sim: duplicate salaries found at the highest salary level
review-note: row offset logic may return the same salary band
explanation: The query first finds the maximum salary, then finds the maximum salary below that value. This treats duplicate top salaries as one distinct salary band.
common_mistakes: Using ROW_NUMBER directly on employee rows
Forgetting that duplicate salaries should not create duplicate ranks
Returning an employee row instead of the salary value
hints: Think in distinct salary bands, not employee rows.
Find the maximum salary lower than the overall maximum salary.
